title: Briam
subtitle: Greek slow-roasted summer vegetable tray with olive oil and tomato.
excerpt: The Greek answer to ratatouille. Courgette, aubergine, potato, onion, pepper, and tomato sliced thin, layered in a wide tray with olive oil and oregano, then roasted slowly until the vegetables collapse into a sweet caramelised stew. Vegan, freezer-friendly, made on the hob-summer days when you want the oven busy.
sourceNotes:
Greek summer home cookery, particularly the islands and the rural Peloponnese where the dish is built around end-of-summer gluts of courgette, aubergine, and tomato. Documented in Greek cookbooks across the twentieth century.

Briam belongs to a wider family of slow-baked Mediterranean vegetable trays that includes Provençal tian and Turkish güveç. The Greek version stands out for its quantity of olive oil and the long unhurried bake.

A Lenten staple by virtue of being vegan; eaten with bread, feta, and olives outside Lent.

content.text: Briam is the Greek summer vegetable tray. Courgette, aubergine, potato, pepper, onion, and tomato, all thinly sliced, layered in olive oil and oregano, baked low and slow until everything collapses into a sweet caramelised stew. The vegetables turn soft and dark at the edges; the tomato and oil pool into a sauce. Vegan by default, freezer-friendly, the kind of pan that gets better on day two.
It is one of the Greek
ladera

, a family of vegetable dishes cooked in plenty of olive oil. The oil is part of the dish. Cut it back and you get a different recipe; cut it back hard and you get roasted vegetables. Briam wants the fat.

The dish is unhurried. Twenty minutes of slicing and the rest is the oven. A wide tray rather than a deep one matters; the vegetables need to sit in a single layer or they steam rather than caramelise.
What you need

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: waxy, peeled, sliced into 3 mm coins

items.name: Tomato
items.prepNote: ripe, half sliced, half grated to a pulp

items.name: Dried oregano
items.prepNote: Greek if you can find it

items.name: Flat-leaf parsley
items.prepNote: leaves picked and chopped, to finish

content.text: Method
Heat the oven and prep the tomato

Heat the oven to 180°C fan, 200°C conventional. Grate half of {{tomato}} of tomatoes through the coarse holes of a box grater into a bowl, discarding the skins as you go. You should have a wet pulp. Slice the rest of the tomatoes into rounds for the top.
Slice everything thin

Cut {{courgette}} courgettes into 5 mm rounds. Slice {{aubergine}} aubergine the same way. Peel {{potato}} of potatoes and slice 3 mm thinner than the courgettes so they finish at the same time. Thinly slice {{onion}} onions and {{pepper-red}} red pepper. Even slicing matters more than perfect slicing; thick chunks cook unevenly.
Layer in the tray

Tip the sliced vegetables into a large bowl. Add the grated tomato pulp, {{garlic}} of sliced garlic, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and a few grinds of black pepper. Mix with your hands until everything is coated. Tip into a wide oven tray and spread into a single layer, no deeper than 4 cm. Arrange the sliced tomato rounds over the top so the surface is covered.
Roast low, with a stir

Slide the tray into the oven on the middle shelf. Roast for ninety minutes total. After thirty minutes, give everything a gentle stir to bring the bottom layer up and reorganise the tomato slices on top. After sixty, check that the potato slices are tender to a knife tip; if they are still firm, give another fifteen minutes.

The dish is done when the vegetables have collapsed, the tomato has gone dark and chutney-like at the edges, and the olive oil and tomato juice pool through the tray. Let it rest ten minutes; briam is meant to be eaten warm rather than hot.
Finish with parsley
Scatter {{parsley-flat}} of chopped flat-leaf parsley over the surface. A final drizzle of olive oil sits well with this. Serve with feta crumbled on top or alongside, country bread for the oil.

attrs.intro: Briam wants slow heat, plenty of oil, and a wide tray. Most failures come from a deep pan, not enough oil, or undercooked potato.

items.fix: Slice potato thinner than the courgette so they finish together; 3 mm is right. Push the oven up to 200°C fan for the last twenty minutes if needed.
items.cause: Potato sliced too thick or oven not hot enough.
items.symptom: Potato still firm at the end.

items.fix: Use a wider tray or split between two trays. The vegetables should sit in a single layer no deeper than 4 cm to caramelise rather than steam.
items.cause: Tray too deep or vegetables piled high.
items.symptom: Vegetables steamed and pale, not caramelised.

items.fix: Briam wants generous oil; cutting back is the most common mistake. Add another two tablespoons at the halfway stir if the tray looks dry.
The oven should be uncovered throughout; the open top lets the moisture evaporate. If the tomatoes are particularly juicy, drain the grated pulp briefly through a sieve before mixing.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting

content.text: Variations

Add a handful of crumbled feta over the top for the last fifteen minutes of cooking for a non-vegan version that some Greek cooks prefer in winter. A sliced fennel bulb in place of the pepper takes the dish in a brighter aniseed direction. Some Aegean cooks add green beans for the last thirty minutes.
Outside summer, this version with autumn squash, onion, and tomato passata in place of the courgette and fresh tomato also works. Different dish in spirit but the same shape and method.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers
Briam is better on day two. Cool completely, refrigerate, and reheat in a 160°C oven covered with foil for fifteen minutes. Four days in the fridge is the safe window.
Freezes well for up to two months. Cool fully and pack into freezer-friendly containers. Defrost in the fridge overnight and reheat in a 180°C oven for fifteen minutes; the edges crisp back up.
Where this dish lives

Briam belongs to the late summer Greek kitchen, when courgettes and aubergines arrive in glut and the tomato vines are bending under their own weight. The dish has its Lenten role too; vegan by default, it is one of the trays that holds up the long stretches of fasting in the Greek Orthodox calendar.

People often compare briam to French ratatouille and to ratatouille's prettier cousin, tian. The family resemblance is real. Briam is the wetter, oil-richer Greek take on a shared Mediterranean idea: use the garden at its summer peak, cook it slowly, eat with bread.
